commentBody:
"I can't find the thin metal strip on my guitar!"
"Fret not!"

("Great, this is just my pluck!")
OK. That one was fast and not really fun. That was a Wednesday just like yesterday. Not even a hard Wednesday.

Sam,

Baltic and Mediterranean are AVES in Atlantic City, as are all the locations found on a standard American Monopoly board.
Easy breezy Friday.  Had a minor detour in the center but still under half my average Friday time…

My only complaint: 41A - EAT IN

Having a meal at a restaurant is eating out, not eating in. Eating in means to dine at home.

Just because someone eats "in" the restaurant doesn't mean they are eating in.

I've always been a lover of the wisdom that comes from philosophy, but I think my first proper introduction came when I got a book at a young age called something like "Zeno's Tortoise and 50 Other Paradoxes." I'm somewhat embarrassed that ELEA was my last answer to fill tonight

I would have enjoyed the food and drink references in this one (TASTETESTER, PANSEAR, MOCHIDONUT, EATIN, TACOBELL, BANGER, TILAPIA, CORNDOGS, GETSLOSHED) much more if wasn’t on day two of a diet that involves no food between meals and limited sweets.

Bands together? for AMFM was clever.
I'm stumped:  which entry was original clued as "Rush delivery?"

The appearance of Zeno makes me think of some lesser known corollaries to his famous paradox:

Zeno’s paradox of the Mochi Donut: since you always have to eat half of what remains, do you ever finish it?

Zeno’s Slomo paradox: you still never get there, but now five times slower.

Zeno’s Free Advice paradox: the harder you try to give it, the less the recipient hears.

Uh oh. The Too easy crew is gonna come out in full force. 

N_SAL made no sense to me.  But fortunately the blank had to be a vowel. 


-Shoutout. MOCHIDONUTS. A bit played out on the left coast.  But if it haven’t tried, you’re welcome. 
-FREEADVICE clue was funny. 
-Very creative with the Baltic and Mediterranean clues. Man. There is no more mind numbing game than monopoly.  

I might argue too many proper nouns, but the crosses were gentle enough. 

Okay. Time to hit 2014 archives

Sam Good Luck on your book, I can't wait for your return. I was afraid you were following Deb outside the fold. Thanks for staying on board, I think I can speak for a few thousand peeps and say we've grown accustomed to your face, your humor and your FAMILIAR expressions we all have when solving zany Cross words. 

I also enjoy your endeavors into strands. I think that is you I've noticed. Good to see you ladies moving throughout the gamesphere  what a fabulous job it must be. 

Congrats on the book.
Not a fan of GET SLOSHED, PAN SEAR, OH NO REASON, or EAT IN. Most of the fill was a gimme and the rest just weren't satisfying. Oh well, beats last Friday by a mile
My FREE ADVICE: eschew the susHI DONUT and go with the MOCHI DONUT.

I ran into trouble in several places. I felt like I was putting out little fires in every quadrant. Eventually, it came down to correctly parsing AMFM to get the job done. Many will likely say it could have been harder, but, it was hard enough for me.

Even though I had a lot of trouble with the bottom middle, the top led me to believe that this was probably fairly easy for the elite solver.  So, sorry.

I enjoyed 31D, even though it reminded me I'm cutting back on the stuff that gets one well lubricated.

Most surprising answer for my money?  40A.  I had NASAL  in mind long before I figured out why it worked.

Not going to harp about the difficulty of this puzzle per se; finally gave me a bit of chewiness in the SE, and pleasant enough I suppose. I appreciated the SEAS/AVES double clue, and enjoyed the clues for MOCHIDONUT, FREEADVICE, and GETSLOSHED.

I will give a more general comment about difficulty level though. I mentioned yesterday that I doubt all the editors are happy with the new normal. As another example, I just finished a brilliant Sunday puzzle from May 1st 2016 by Joel Fagliano (one of the editors) and Byron Walden. Try this one out and tell me with a straight face that this level of challenge and sophistication in theme, grid, and clueing would be replicated in a Sunday puzzle today.

Carolyn and Brian,

I love your clue [Rush delivery?] for INDUCE. Ha! Been there, done that! 

Thanks for the fun puzzle!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: 2020 wants its Wednesday back.
Another quickie.  Even a little faster than yesterday's.
25 A makes little sense. They never sponsored the series, only cars.
One of the easiest Friday puzzles ever.

Personally I found this hard but doable - my solve time was exactly my Friday average. My across run yielded almost nothing. The down pass was more fruitful, yet I needed many more passes to fill the grid. Dozens of guesses were involved, and I encountered very few gimmes.

Apparently being Polish is one of the solutions to finding some recent NYT crossword puzzles too easy. Had this run on a Wednesday, as suggested below, I'd have been annoyed, personally. 

The crossing of R_C Nation and GETSL_SHED was the perfect example of the sort of difficulties I face, and which Americans might not even notice. Slang crossing with a proper name is just cruel for a solver like me.
Side note on 27A: I have the soundtrack to Li'l Abner on vinyl.

What city are Mediterranean and Baltic Avenues in? Why, Atlantic City NJ of course. That's where most of the property names in Monopoly come from.

(I was just lucky I thought of avenues first and seas second.)

userLocation: Somewhere in the North Sea, The Hague
commentBody:
Unusual solve today.

Our hotel is basically in the North Sea. 
On the North Sea..?

The walls are glass and (other than our private porch)
our entire view is water. 
So, getting out of bed is tough, because 
the view is heaven and utterly breathtaking.

So- hubby sat next to me as I solved.

That. Doesn’t. Happen.

But travel is essentially romantic,
and this ridiculous view…

He was even interested, 
and my old historian loved TORE for [ Rent ].

He’s not hooked, but he peeked through
a little window into this word world and 
I got to enjoy my puzzle with him!

Huzzah!

(Don’t know the Dutch word because after learning
how to say “Thank you very much”
we gave up.
Even the response to “thank you”
has 27 syllables and requires a bit of spitting.)

Happy Friday all!

How do I get the puzzle app to go back to opening the column in the regular nyt app the way it used to? When I read the comments I often want to go look at the puzzle but now I can't without losing my place!

Great clue [Rush delivery?] for INDUCE . I had to look at the comments to figure it out though, which is odd because I'm a doula and I've been to many many inductions.

Inductions are rushed in the sense of starting labour early, but they tend to be long affairs, when measured as time spent in the hospital. They have also become very very common. The reasons for that are varied, and not always health-related.

I wondered if I was on the constructor's wavelength, which is why I solved this in Wednesday time, or if it was just that easy. The comments to date tell me that it was indeed too easy.

To any NYT staff reading this comment, if this trend continues, I will not be renewing my subscription.
[Rush delivery?] has my vote.  The editors get a disappointed shake of my head for rejecting it.

If I were to be fussy (ok, I'm going to be fussy), I'd say that there's nothing more "natural" about green energy than there is about crude oil. Oil is an organic substance for goodness sake!

Other organic things:
- Plastic

Other natural things:
- Murder

Hey, if you can't get fussy about words in a crossword comments section, where can you?!
Not in all the millennia since Zeno would it have dawned on me that there was such a thing as a chia pet or what it was.

Υμνος has no SILENTN 

I certainly don’t pronounce hymn him

Loved this a lot! Some great clueing and solid long answers. 

My only nit is the dupe usage of the “CORN whole?” clue, when we have CORN DOGS as an answer. No bueno, and so easily avoided.
ROC/SHO/CHIA extremely obscure for international solvers I think 🙂... CHIA I had to look up after the solve because I didn't understand
Can anyone explain how "rent" means "tore"? I was able to fill it in but still do not understand

I still wish we had the progressive difficulty through the week that we used to get. Maybe I’m grumpy from lack of sleep. My daughter had her wisdom teeth extracted this week and my sleep has been wackadoodle like the newborn days… 🫩
I liked the duplicate clues of Baltic/Mediterranean. I think Rushed Delivery was a far superior clue for a Friday. Overall I enjoyed the solve, I just would have enjoyed it more with some elevated clueing. 
And now off for our very late spring break 🎉

A nice chewy Friday. For those of you who thought it too easy, may I stick up for us International solvers, who will always be flummoxed by the standard US only names, companies, foodstuffs etc etc. Fighting my way through those quagmires can be interesting.
Got into 37A tv show quite late, but thoroughly, um, is ?enjoyed the right word? 
42D is the reason I can’t drink wine of any hue. Plays havoc with my system. Fortunately tea isn’t an issue otherwise I’d have to hand in my passport.
Is BAG IT a term for quitting or does it just mean putting a towel in a bag?
Not in my wheelhouse. Never heard of a MOCHIDONUT, which made the south difficult. Apparently, I need to read less and watch more TV and movies, as much of the pop culture was unkown to me.
It’s on me for solving without my glasses, but, boy, did I tie myself in knots wondering how an autopsy could stave off a LATEFEE.
Why is it so hard for me to access the Midi? First, it won't even show up, and when it does, it will say, "Page no longer available."
That was interesting because at first go it felt like a Wednesday but there were tricky spots in there that were definitely a Friday.

Highlights:
•	FRET NOT – I’ve never used this before, but it has such a lovely aura of kindness, that I’m going to try.
•	MOCHI – Don’t know about the donut version, but I find mochi melt-in-your-mouth addictive, and it’s fun to pan fry because of how it balloons up.  Very worth a try.
•	Pop in the grid today, with seven answers never seen in the Times puzzle before, including the entire triple stack in the middle, and my favorite answer, OH, NO REASON.
•	I like how DOWNER goes from north to south.
•	Semordnilap heaven!  Not only the rare-in-crosswords five-letter semordnilap SERIF, but IN OT right under TONI.
•	I love getting misdirected as I did with NASAL clued as a noun, and BROWN clued as a verb.

That’s a lotta like in a little box – thank you for this, Carolyn and Brian!
That was a really speedy Friday. The only thing that slowed me down was a MOCHI DONUT, had me in a sticky situation.

Way too easy.

BSHARP is not equal to C. 

Another horrible puzzle in a line going back 2 years now..
Some cute clues, but this one won’t do much to quiet the “puzzles getting too easy” crowd.
The Mini has a clue for "basketball player, informally" and the correct answer is missing a letter.
I had to double check I wasn't on an archived puzzle, Sprint Cup hasn't been a thing since 2016.

Given the U.S.-specific crossing answers, I'd say international solvers comprise much of the 7% noted below in the xwstats.com summary.

 Global Stats
Difficulty  Easy
Median Solve Time  10:31
Median Solver  30% faster
⚡93% of users solved faster than their Friday average.
72% solved much faster (>20%) than their Friday average.
🐢7% of users solved slower than their Friday average.
3% solved much slower (>20%) than their Friday average.
Nice work!  Very much appreciated for a Friday puzzle.

*SIGHS*

Completed in less than half my Friday average, even though I at one point walked away from the computer with the timer running.

Listen. I am not rich. I am a single woman supporting myself on a lower-middle-class budget. I cannot afford to be paying for this crossword if I'm not enjoying it.

I feel like they published the Easy Mode by mistake.

This might have made for a nice themeless Wednesday (or Tuesday), but, c'mon, it ain't no Friday.
No teeth whatsoever.  A smooth and easy solve.
That's not what I come for on a Friday.

I might share some of the clues I found amusing, but I'm not in the mood.  Instead I'll share the one that would have been nice on a Wednesday, but not on a Friday:  "International fast-food chain without a location in Mexico, interestingly".  Essentially this clue says "Mexican chain that's not truly Mexican".  Hmmm, let me think.
"Ticks of a ticker" is nicely stated, but also a gimme.
I could go on, but I'm beginning to bore myself.

C'mon, NYT puzzle editors, do better!

I’m not saying that I *owned* this puzzle.
But today I rent through it in a hurry.

(As: under one twainth the usual time)

READY, SET, GO and I'm off to the races,
A couple of HEARTBEATS; I've filled all the spaces.
THE LAST OF US finished in lickety-split.
Look forward to Fridays, but this wasn't it.
Good job you kids – this was a fun workout with some really good clues. I hope to see you here again.

Well, I'm always bringing up the rear, but still surprised that so many found this one to be an unusually easy Friday. Typical tough workout for me, and had to look up a couple of things. And... even after I finished I can see at least 5 answers that are completely unfamiliar terms to me. No big deal. Puzzle find - A Sunday from October 16, 1994 by Raymond Hamel with the title: "Out of their element." Some theme answers: FLYINTHEOINTMENT CATCHERINTHERYE BATSINTHEBELFRY DOGINTHEMANGER CASTLEINTHAIR BULLINACHINASHOP Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/16/1994&g=44&d=D ....

I enjoyed this puzzle.  Yes, it was a tad easier than the usual Friday, as I didn't need the 'Easy Mode' clues.  I only had one error:  "C's equivalent" is "B Sharp", not D sharp (crossing 'Bic' (products often coming with caps) makes more sense, too.)  But it was a fun puzzle, and I don't feel cheated.

Best of luck with your book, Sam!  I look forward to announcement of its publication via the Blue Sky.

Just way too easy.  Given how much competition there is for themeless puzzle acceptances, every time a Friday or Saturday comes in like this feels like even more of a missed opportunity.

Considering checking xwstats difficulty before I solve, so at least my expectations are managed better.  That may be the path to crossword serenity.

This seemed very "gettable" except where it wasn't!  A lot of "Not My Area" clues (especially 37A, for example) and 31D's GET SL????ED.  SLAMMED?  The crosses of 47A and 53A were zero help.
Just learned about MOCHI very recently (Jeopardy! And maybe a cooking show)...

My favorite clue/answer was 29D!
It reminded me of a biography of Margery of Kemp, a medieval woman who made a pilgrimmage to The Holy Land.  She could read and write (quite unusual for the times!) and described many personal conversations with Christ, who often said to her, "FRET thee NOT, Dowter."  

Okay, I guess that's my divergent comment for the day.

Since the puzzle provided little amusement, I’ll amuse myself (and maybe one or two others) by relating Zeno’s proof that motion doesn’t exist.  Some of you may know his “Achilles and the Tortoise” proof, but I prefer “Zeno’s Arrow”.

Imagine a movie of an arrow in flight.  In each frame of the movie, that is, in each moment of time, the arrow isn’t moving.  So, when is it moving?
You might say that it moves between frames.  But between frames, there are only more moments, and in each of those moments, the arrow isn’t moving.
You might point out that, the arrow actually is moving in each frame, because, no matter how good the camera, there is duration to the shutter speed, and the arrow must be moving then, if only a little bit.
But that’s only because the camera shutter isn’t truly a “moment”.  If it were, the arrow would be perfectly still.
In short, time is made up solely of moments, and in each moment, the arrow is not moving, so motion is impossible,  QED.

Pretty nice proof, no?
It doesn’t have to be difficult to be a Friday. Fridays are themeless.

A very rare Friday golden star.  Fun puzzle. I thought SLOMO should have been clued better, like maybe "Like many NFL replays" so you know it's abbreviated or maybe "informally", at the end. Otherwise two thumbs up. Time to get lubricated! Cheers.

Well, I got some of it. As some have mentioned, obscure to the non US. I looked up CORN DOGS and don't think I'm missing much, TACO BELL or MOCHI DONUTS either. Not that British cuisine has such a lot to boast about. 




PAN SEAR, SERIF were nice clues, and SILENT N. Couldn't we have more (most?) clues like that?

I have never paid any attention to who 'airs' a TV programme. Why does it matter? I have watched hundreds of episodes of Law and Order/CSI/NCIS  but have no idea which company produced them or 'aired' them.
Love the shout-out to my favorite Sino-Spanglish rockers, The Las Tofus!

nice & chewy for a friday, had a lot of fun with this one (even if i solve crosswords like i have never seen a single movie or know anyone who would ever be in one). in other news, i finally hit 2400 NYT Crosswords officially Solved this week! so it's just onward & upward to 3000 from here. cheers & happy solving!

I flew through this puzzle at a Tuesday-level clip....until I came to a screeching halt in the SE. If only the entire puzzle could have been a similar challenge level...

On the whole, an enjoyable journey.

Was well on my way to a Friday personal best (and probably by a significant margin) until I got modestly bogged down in the middle south of the puzzle.  Never had a MOCHIDONUT and never heard of one either.  AMFM, CEL and the “sloshed” of GETSLOSHED crossing that darned donut didn’t help.  But ultimately it all came together and still at less than half my average time.

While my personal best streak continues, my satisfaction with the accomplishment diminishes with every “too easy” puzzle that is published.  I know, be careful what you wish for but, with the odd exception, there has been a very notable decline in the difficulty of these puzzles starting maybe 5 or 6 months ago (not coincidentally around the same time that my current streak began) and I, for one, would prefer stricter tests.  My current streak is 66% better than my previous best from about two years ago, but I don’t think I’ve gotten 66% better.  It’s a close call, but my previous streak may have been more fulfilling.  I know that “Games”  have become a major revenue driver for the NYT, and I suppose the thinking is that participation/revenue can be grown further via more “accessible” puzzles.   But at some point the brand is diminished, and I fear that tipping point may soon be at hand.

As I flew through the NW, I was dreading another round of commentary on overly easy Fridays. And then I got to the rest of the puzzle. 

I still finished much below my average, but only after a struggle. In the NE, I thought the composer/actor cross was an utter natick, but after getting the crosses (and giving up on the poetic/quasi-spiritual idea that all hymns end in SILENce), the one remaining letter at the cross wasn't hard to guess.

Loved seeing LASTOFUS and MOCHIDONUTS, both of which I have a complex relationship with--and the cluing for TACOBELL was perfect. Thanks constructors & eds. for an enjoyable diversion! 

Now I'm off to Google Zeno of ELEA (and maybe "fillip" as well) in a final procrastinatory fling before getting to work.

One more puzzle find, thanks to something popping into my head. Wondered about some answer that might substitute FAX for FACTS and... yep - a Wednesday from August 10, 1994 by Ernie Furtado. Some theme answers: JUSTTHEFAXMAAM DOUBLELOX QUARTERBACKSAX Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/10/1994&g=20&d=A ....
Was the original cluing, ‘rush delivery’ for the answer ‘SLOMO’ ?

I see that many people thought this was too easy. Good for them. I had a lovely time. I did come in under my average, but not by too much, and I enjoyed myself. Clues like MOCHIDONUT and TORE had me chewing for a minute and I had fun. I haven’t been doing puzzles for years, and I’m still finding my wings with Friday puzzles in particular. For that reason, it felt nice to have a victory and still enjoy myself without needing to look at the Friday Easy clues.
Death, taxes, and wordplay commenters bemoaning the puzzle being too easy.

OK for all the -" it's too easy for a Friday " folks . I am a veteran solver - my current streak is over 6 years ( crazy I know ) . I am not young . I have never had a MOCHI DONUT , was unfamiliar with ROC nation , and I thought some of the cueing in this one was terrific - Bands together for AMFM ? Awesome . I am not obsessed at all with level of difficulty but this one made me think a bit and I love that . Enjoy the ride team and harken back to the archive if you need more !

Newbie here. I got through this one pretty well. I had to look up ROC, ALDEN, SHO, and THELASTOFUS. There is a fun scene in the current movie "Song Sung Blue" where Kate Hudson and Hugh Jackman (playing Claire and Mike Sardina) are eating CORNDOGS at a carnival. I got this DVD from the library and have been watching it over and over, along with the writer/director commentary. Claire and Mike formed a Neil Diamond Interpreter duo--in real life. It's a biopic. Lots of nostalgia. They opened for Pearl Jam, with Edie Vedder. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt30343021/ The movie was inspired by a documentary about the duo. You can see the real Eddie Vedder in it. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YDukMQIz1Sc If I had been asked about Eddie Vedder in a puzzle, that would have been another lookup for me. I have heard of Pearl Jam, though.
Years ago, I watched tacos being made in the street of Tijuana. They could attract more Gringos, by having a Taco Bell there.

I don’t have all their sheet music, but surely Rush must have delivered the occasional B-sharp in a few of their songs.

What do I win? Or was there no inducement?
Feeling like I would like to see more of the original unedited cluing for this puzzle. For those of you struggling with mochi…it is made from glutinous rice…see one the other is sure to follow.

Difficult one today. 

I didn't love the cluing. I found it to be a little too opaque, and when the answers came (if they did)I had several "I suppose?" moments in my head. 

"Friday clues can be merciless in their ambiguity." -- Indeed, Sam. Some will cheer for the ambiguity, stating that's what puzzles should be, and others will bemoan how apparently easy they found it. That's fine. 

I don't think puzzles should be difficult through ambiguity alone. The constructor should act as a guide rather a guard to the puzzle's completion, and this particular puzzle felt more like the former than the latter. Again, I know some people will say that it should be that way, and to that I say: different strokes. 

Unfortunately, today was an unsatisfying slog. 

It's a beautiful day here, and I'm going enjoy the blue skies and sunshine. 

Happy Friday.

For months I’ve been playing the Friday “easy mode” puzzle first to build my crossword vocabulary and solving skills… and today I solved the regular puzzle without it! 

(I can see from the comments that several folks think today’s was too easy for a Friday, but I’m gonna take the W. Sometimes I get stuck on a Tuesday and fly through a Thursday — and I don’t think I’ve ever finished a Friday completely unaided before. Here’s to learning! 🥂 Happy solving to all!)
An easy, breezy 4:50 puzzle. Perfect Wednesday!
Enjoyable Friday puzzle! Well done!
Got stuck on HEAR_BEATS and S_P … it was hard to “hear” anything that sounded right aha
Everybody seems to be assuming "Rush Delivery" was intended for INDUCE.  My vote is for ACID.  Somebody please tell me I'm wrong.

I was sure that m amd n had to refer to DASHes...somehow...DASHS?
Then I got it.
I would like to correct a slight error in the Sprint Cup Series clue… it hasn’t been called that for about ten years, it’s now just the NASCAR Cup Series

I set a Friday personal best today.  Fun clues, all the proper nouns had more figure-outable crossings.  It helped that there were a few of the longer clues that were instantly gettable like THE LAST OF US and HEARTBEATS.

Solved this without help, but not easily. Felt like a Friday puzzle to me, and took more than thirty minutes to complete.

Most of my time was spent in the center of the grid. BAGIT didn't connect with its clue for me, the HBO series was an unknown, as was ROC as clued. "One offering biting critiques?" for TASTETESTER also slowed me down, and even with the question mark seems a bit dopey. Having GETSMASHED instead of GETSLOSHED didn't help either.

The rest of the puzzle went pretty smoothly, and, overall, this seemed like a solid and appropriately challenging Friday puzzle.

I must admit that I find the search function very useful.  Although I can't search my name, I can search my location.  This brings up all my posts and tells me if anyone has replied to them.

If your location is just "New York," it might be a little more difficult to search that way. But so far, it seems like I'm the only one from Haverstraw who comments here.

Good workout.  Couple of entries tied me up for a while, DRAW instead of HINT and LAPS in lieu of LANE.  The NASAL clue was clever.  Did not know MOCHIDONUT.  Had to look up a few in this one.  Well constructed puzzle.
I may be blanking…but can someone explain how Rent comes to TORE?  I figured it out with the crosses but still unsure of how those are related?
‘Rush delivery” = Nasal.  I can think of many more appropriate adjectives that better describe this late hate-monger, but I will leave it there.

Very fun puzzle, and I laughed out loud at [It might be worth just as much as you pay for it]; last corner to fall for me. I did not know the fusion pastry, but with only the first letter left to fill in, I could figure it out thanks to [Bands together?], which I also thought was very clever. Thanks, Carolyn Davies Lynch and Brian Callahan!
good challenging Friday. I don't remember musical keys, but I guess B Sharp was supposed to be an easy fill. Today I learned. I like the shoutout to green energy.

Finished in a few seconds shy of a Friday personal record, delayed mostly due to having TORN instead of TORE at 52D for awhile. 

As an amateur pianist, I hate the key of B SHARP, which has the maximum number of sharps (seven). My key mnemonics:

Sharps: Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle
Flats: Battle Ends And Down Goes Charles' Father
Rush delivery? Presumably 43 down, INDUCE.

Mochi donuts are a thing?! 🤯 That just made my day. New side quest unlocked. I must find a mochi donut. 
Thank you for a fun puzzle! The NE corner was tough for me, until CORNDOGS finally came to me (but I will not be having one with my mochi donut).

1 Across --  I have had a few copies of Abbie Hoffman's "Steal This Book" pass through my shelves.  I never bought one nor did I begrudge the friends who "owned" them after me.  Lifted is the verb I was looking for.

"Rent" is past tense. "Torn" is the correct response.
If he wanted "tore" the clue should have been "rend".
Where are the editors?
Corn dogs, bangers, donuts and Taco Bell - I'm headed to Statin Island. To be counseled by my concerned relative Auntie Oxidant. Who will push the chia and tilapia. And forbid the sloshing.

Rush delivery is a perfect clue for induce! At least for my generation of mothers, the only salient meaning of “induce” is the one related to “delivery” as in, L&D. What percentage of solvers- and editors- have given birth? 🤔 Mochi donuts are delicious- Third Culture Bakery in Berkeley is the original as far as I’m concerned- and interestingly, gluten free! Fun puzzle :)

A bit fast for a Friday, but generally enjoyable.

I got stuck on rent torn for a bit, but still finished fairly quickly (for me).

AM/FM are "bands together"? The AM band is from  540–1700 kHz. The FM band is from  87.5 MHz to 108 MHz. They are separated by 85.8 MHz. Hardly "together".

As a retired engineer, I find such wordplay bizarre and mostly inscrutable. It would be nice if the puzzle editor paid attention to the scientific basis of a clue. However as a relative "newby" to crossword solving, I suppose the "?" was the absolution.

Just my $0.02 (2.00% of one American dollar).
Note for the editor of Easy Mode - 39D - B Sharp is the equivalent of C Natural.  The equivalent of A Flat is G Sharp.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ i am a trace
a brief offering at the apogee
of the walrus traced in secrecy
i influence company
i am the instrument the document the words a dash
i am d/ a british food warning
about the existence of space
a/ the last taste
[a gasp]
Mind-bending.

A Flap over Fillip

I had never bothered to look up a definition for FILLIP before this morning.
Plodding through the clues, I bumped into 
28A:
A writer’s fillip.
Huh. What could that be? A favorite turn of phrase? A natty scarf? A treasured rhyme? 
You know, some unnecessary enhancement?

So I looked up on-line and off and was startled.
A FILLIP turns up as a snarky little gesture, a minor physical assault. The sudden sharp straightening of an index finger curled against the thumb. 

That particular way to annoy your sibling in the backseat on a long car trip HAS A NAME.  Jeepers. Nothing to do with a delicate shape extending graceful  typography.  What the hell? Give a FILLIP to a writer — they won’t like it.

My usual late puzzle find. A Thursday from July 24, 2014 by David Phillips. The 'reveal' in that one was... PAINTITBLACK You might be able to guess the trick, but will just note that there were three other theme answers that included the word - IT. Saw ABBIE Hoffman speak when he visited my campus senior year. Later that evening, I was already tucked in when I heard a seeming crowd rush in, and my somewhat kooky roommate knocked on my door and announced that Mr. H was among the group. I had committed to my bedtime and decided to eschew the gathering (partly because roommate was not the most reliable source of information). The visit was confirmed the next day , however, and I’ve regretted missing that pretty unique opportunity.

@Greg Anderson

Dining in and eating in are not used in the same way. 

Dining in is the opposite of taking out. It is a question a hostess at the restaurant might ask you: "Are we dining in or taking out tonight?"

You would never go to a restaurant and get asked by the hostess "Are we eating in or eating out tonight?" That would be a very awkward sentence. 

Eating in means one thing - having a meal at home.

@Steve L I'm in agreement that the clue is perfectly reasonable. However, as I understand it B# is not necessarily the same pitch as C. On a piano or other equally-tempered instrument they are the same by necessity, but in just intonation where the intervals are based on nice frequency ratios in the harmonic series there will be a difference as I understand it. String instruments without frets are more flexible in this regard. So, a violinist playing a piece in C# major would probably finger B# in a slightly different place on a string to where they would finger C in a C major piece, for example, because it would sound more harmonious to their ear. Apologies to any string players out there if I have misunderstood this!

@Dc

B# is identical to tone and frequency to C natural. I get what you’re saying, but this is a crossword puzzle (maybe just a crossword, since it wasn’t very puzzling), not a music theory exam.

@The X-Phile

I’ve always understood Xeno’s Arrow to be that to get from point A to point B, you must first travel half the distance. Then, to get from that new point A to point B, you must first travel half the distance. And so on and so on, always traveling half the distance so never arriving.

@The X-Phile

Huh, just looked it up and found I never actually learned Zeno’s Arrow, just another one of his paradoxes under the wrong name.

The X-Phile,

I would only admit it proves that arrows don’t move, if you threatened me at arrow-point.
:)

@The X-Phile

Another similar paradox is Achilles and the Tortoise. Achilles is running a race with a tortoise. He gives the tortoise a 10 meter head start. At some point Achilles runs that 10 meters and at that point the Tprtoise must be some finite point past that; say, a meter. Now Achilles makes up that meter but at the point the tortoise must be some finite point past that. And so on. So how does Achilles ever catch up to the tortoise?

Or, you shoot an arrow 100 meters. To get there it must first go 50 meters. To get there it must first go 25 meters. Etc. If space is is infinitely divisible then there is always a distance it must cross before it gets to the next one. So how does it move?

But you are correct, you expressed Zeno’s original paradox the most accurately.

@Joe

This is the more formal version of the proof that is known as "Achilles and the Tortoise".

@The X-Phile 

As the length of time decreases, the amount of movement approaches zero, so the limit is zero. But a period of time that has no length, implied by the term "a moment," makes no sense.

@SP

Zeno created a number of paradoxes based on whether or not time and space were "infinitely divisible".  The Arrow is just my favorite.

In Plato's dialogue "Parmenides", Socrates, then a young man, questions the old Monist from Elea and his prize student Zeno.

It's an extremely difficult dialogue, but, like all of Plato's dialogues, there's some playfulness as well.
At one point, Socrates says to Zeno, "So, if I understand you correctly, you've created *many* proofs that only *the One* exists."

As Samuel Johnson would say 2,000 years later, kicking a stone, "I refute it thus."

@edyonkers

Whose perspective?  At what time?

@Bruce

Zeno gives a version of this paradox for space, as well.  

Space is composed exclusively of an infinite number of points, but each point has zero dimension; that is, it's nothing.  And if you add nothing to nothing -- even if you do this an infinite number of times -- the result is still nothing.
So, space does not exist.

Such are the paradoxes of the infinite and the infinitesimal.

@Joe You are correct. As a fifth century BCE philosopher, Zeno would of course only have had access to still cameras, so this movie camera analogy would have made no sense to him.

(/s in case necessary in these humorless times, but in any case take it from a professional classicist, you are correct)

@The X-Phile

@sarahgrin, I've stopped (if indeed I ever did) caring about what others think of as easy/hard/appropriate day of the week for puzzles. It's all so very individual and experience based, and I don't mean just solving experience. Life experience matters too!

I've been solving NYT and other major papers' crosswords for about 45 years. When even younger, I solved TV Guide and lesser newspapers' puzzles, plus the ubiquitous Penny Press found at supermarket checkouts. In other words, I've been a crossword fanatic and word nerd nearly all of my life. I'm a skilled solver by any standard. And guess what? I still get stumped. Themes escape me. Pop culture is hit or miss. I often find easy a puzzle others have labeled hard, and vice versa. There's no rhyme or reason sometimes, and I've decided I simply don't care if someone else feels it's hard or easy or should be somehow different. 

It's a hobby that I enjoy immensely, and I read and enjoy the comments while doing my best not to get riled up by the!

@DH — (Constructor here) — yes, Third Culture made the mochi muffins sold at my local boba shop years ago, and I developed quite an addiction! Sooo tasty. And they were at the center of the mochi muffin trademark war we mentioned in the constructor notes. And glad you liked our clue! :)

@Disgusted in America You were right to note the question mark. In this case, it directs us to a very literal interpretation of the clue. The term “AM/FM” literally puts those two disparate bands together.
The clue is about user experience, not engineering. They are "bands together" on every AM/FM radio I have owned (and on those you have owned?).
N.B. The question mark on the clue is a wordplay signal (did we really need it on a Friday?) to not expect a most expected meaning of the phrase (in which bands would be a verb).
